clusters.title: Tropical Cyclone Narelle could strengthen into a category 5 before landfall
clusters.neutral_headline: Tropical Cyclone Narelle Approaches Queensland Coast

items.title: Tropical Cyclone Narelle could strengthen into a category 5 before landfall
items.standfirst: Tropical Cyclone Narelle is expected to cross the Queensland coast as a category 5 system, bringing heavy to intense rainfall and strong winds.
items.content: Tropical Cyclone Narelle is expected to strengthen into a category 5 system before it crosses the Queensland coast on Friday morning.The current category 4 system is expected to bring heavy to intense rainfall and strong winds to coastal regions, including communities between Lockhart River and Cape Melville.Bureau of Meteorology senior Meteorologist Christie Johnson said the cyclone reached a category 3 system late on Wednesday afternoon and strengthened further to a category 4 system overnight.LIVE: Trump lashes out at Australia, allies over lack of help"The system is currently located well off the Queensland coast, about 500km east-north-east of Cooktown and will continue to move west today heading for the Queensland coast," Johnson said."A coastal crossing is expected Friday morning."At this stage most likely somewhere between Lockhart River and Cape Melville."The tropical cyclone is forecast to be a category 4 storm as it crosses the coast. "From there, the severe tropical cyclone will continue west while weakening, likely dropping back to a category 2 system by the time it crosses the west coast of the cape yorke peninsula on Friday night," Johnson said."It will then track across the Gulf of Carpenteria on Saturday morning, heading for the Northern Territory and likely intensifying into a severe tropical cyclone."It will then move across the Northern Territory during the weekend."A tropical cyclone warning is current for coastal areas between Lockhart Ri ver and Cape Tribulation as well as adjacent inland parts of the Cape York Peninsula."These areas are likely to see gale force winds and damaging to destructive wind gusts developing during today, with the strongest winds most likely from tonight," Johnson said."As the cyclone crosses the coast we could see peak very destructive winds in excess of 230km/h close to the core of the system, with broader areas of destructive wind gusts up to 160km/h between Lockhart River and Cooktown."Heavy to intense rainfall will also build in this tropical cyclone warning area through today, more likely from tonight continuing into Friday morning."Flash flooding will become an increasing concern as the cyclone draws near."Ab normally high tides, which may lead to inundation of low-lying areas, are expected to develop along the far north Queensland coast as Narelle approaches and crosses the coast.A d angerous storm tide is forecast around Princess Charlotte Bay, particularly if the system crosses the coast around the time of the high tide on Friday morning.There is a broader tropical cyclone watch area that extends west across the Cape York Peninsula, covering areas between Weipa and Kowanyama on the western or Gulf coast."These areas are likely to see winds and rain increasing, particularly on Friday," Johnson said."We are likely to see the tropical cyclone warning area extending to these areas during the day."As we go into the weekend, the impact from several days of rain will also be felt and riverine flooding is likely across much of Cape York.READ MORE: Government won't rule out fuel rationing as war drags on" Flood watches are in place for the entirety of the Cape York Peninsula with river rises likely over the weekend."Outside of the tropical cyclone warning area, there is also a gale-force wind warning for the Cairns coastal waters today."There is also a chance that severe thunderstorms could develop on the edge of the system and bring localised rainfall and heavy flooding to parts of the north tropical coast, north of about Ingham," Johnson said." If this occurs, then we will issue severe thunderstorm warnings for those thunderstorms."The top end of the Northern Territory is likely to see impacts building over the weekend."At this stage Tropical Cyclone Narelle looks likely to reach the east coast of the Top End late Saturday and into Sunday as a severe tropical cyclone, before moving across southern parts of the Top End Sunday into Monday," Johnson said." We're likely to see the risk of damaging winds, heavy rainfall and flash flooding increasing over eastern parts of the Top End through Saturday as the system approaches and extending westwards on Sunday and Monday."NEVER MISS A STORY: Get your breaking news and exclusive stories first by following us across all platforms.Download the 9NEWS App here via Apple and Google PlayMake 9News your preferred source on Google by ticking this box hereSign up to our breaking newsletter here

clusters.title: The comments from Trump's right-hand man that should annoy every Aussie driver
clusters.neutral_headline: Trump Aide Comments On Petrol Prices

items.title: The comments from Trump's right-hand man that should annoy every Aussie driver
items.standfirst: JD Vance has told an audience other countries were "suffering" on petrol prices worse than America.
items.content: In a speech that would offer little comfort to Australians, US Vice President JD Vance has told an audience other countries were "suffering" on petrol prices worse than America.Petrol prices have shot up worldwide in the wake of the US war on Iran. Here in Australia, the average cost of unleaded petrol is now $2.29/litre and diesel up to $2.71/litre.But things are worse "overseas", Vance said at a rally in Detroit.READ MORE: Trump berates allies as he finds himself alone on Iran war "So as much as we've got to focus on getting these gas prices down, the reality is, overseas they're feeling it far worse than we did because we've taken the steps to protect our energy economy."Vance appeared to blame US allies for not focusing enough on securing fossil fuels."We actually worked with a lot of our allies all over the world who are suffering from this frankly more than we are because unlike the United States where we pursued an energy dominance agenda," he said."You've got a lot of people all over the world who have focused on a lot of green energy scams and they're hurting a lot more than we are."Vance spoke of "working with allies" to release hundreds of millions of barrels out of petroleum reserves to put downward pressure on prices.READ MORE: Donald Trump says he'll be 'taking Cuba' in latest escalation Australia was one such country to release petrol from its reserves, though it has not appeared to bring prices down.In the US, a litre of petrol costs about A$1.44. That's about 80 cents cheaper than average prices at the bowser in Australia.As a net oil exporter, the US has not seen panic buying at petrol stations like in Australia.But Americans are still feeling the pinch after seeing petrol prices surge.In his speech, Vance pointed to high prices under the Biden administration."(Petrol prices are) not even as high as they were during certain parts of the Biden administration because of what's going on in the Middle East," he said."It's not going to last forever."READ MORE: The four-letter acronym that could pull Trump out of IranYesterday Donald Trump's top economic advisor said The pain felt by consumers as a result of the war with Iran is "the last of our concerns". National Economic Council Director Kevin Hassett told CNBC that the war with Iran "wouldn't really disrupt the US economy very much at all"."It would hurt consumers and we'd have to think about if that continued what we would have to do about that," Hassett said."But that's the like, really the last of our concerns right now because we're very confident that this thing is going ahead of schedule."US inflation data released overnight was twice the anticipated figure. Petrol prices are anticipated to rise further with a substantial jump in oil prices today.READ MORE: 'Dead by June': Trump announces congressman's private diagnosisNEVER MISS A STORY: Get your breaking news and exclusive stories first by following us across all platforms.Download the 9NEWS App here via Apple and Google PlayMake 9News your preferred source on Google by ticking this box hereSign up to our breaking newsletter here
